title: 2026/04/23 EDSI [Part 1]
displayTitle: 2026-04-22 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Kordia Group Limited [Part 1]
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Kordia Group Limited
description: Thursday 23 April 2026 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Kordia Group Limited Kordia Group - Neil Livingston, CEO - Sophie Haslem, Chair of the Board - Jason Fullerton-Smith, Executive General Manager - Specialist Solutions
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Kordia Group Limited. Kordia Group. Neil Livingston, CEO

chunks.text: Good morning. Good morning. Good morning. Thank you so much.

So, good morning. We have half an hour with you. So I believe it's Sophie Haslam. Is that correct? Yes, that's right. Yeah. Thank you so much, and your team. So if you want to open with some remarks, and then we can have questions from members. Thank you. Thank you so much. So I'm Sophie Haslam, I'm the chair of Cordia. On this side, I have Neil Livingstone, who is the CEO.

He's just gone through two years in that position. And Jason Fullingtons Smith, who is the executive general manager of specialized solutions. He's been with Cordia for 15 years, so any really technical, complex questions, he'll cover us for that one. And I think Neil's going to open with some remarks just to talk about the year in question for you.

Thank you. Yes. I thought I'd just take a little bit of time, not too long, just to sort of paint a little bit of the picture of FY25. It was a really important year for Cordia, and I think a little bit of context, which probably doesn't necessarily come through in the annual report might be helpful and just help the discussion.

So if I was to summarize FY25 for us, it really was a year of change and transformation. It was quite a unusual year for Cordia in that we took a lot of decisions. We took some bold decisions, and we made some key changes to the company, which is going to really set us up for the future.

We spent quite a lot of time really thinking through what the purpose of Cordia is and what our core purpose and what we deliver to both New Zealand and our shareholder and make sure that that's top of mind. And we really, really spent a lot of time thinking about how do we return to our financial targets, how do we cut our costs to meet our revenues and make sure that we hit our numbers, because that's incredibly important.

Cordia is an organization which does many things. So I thought I'd just take a couple seconds just to sort of briefly tell you the main highlights, because it's not a company people are typically sort of knowledgeable about. Media and broadcast, so that's our history. That's where we came from. We've been in that business for sixty-five years through different names, through NZBC and eventually rebranding ourself to Cordia.

But the broadcast industry, delivering TV and radio signals is really where we came through as an organization, and that still flows through as to who we are and at our essence, what we do. We are technical experts.The next significant area we've spent a lot of time focusing on and have a relatively large team on is around cybersecurity.

So we look after cybersecurity for our customers, both protecting them but also helping them if they unfortunately get breached, with an incident response team. And help them not just with the technical aspects, but also how do they just manage internally, how do they manage the communications. One area of Cordia that not many people know that we do is around our maritime space. So we have two maritime operation centers, one in Canberra, Australia, and one here in Wellington.

And between those two, we look after about a quarter of the world's oceans from a safety of life perspective. So what that means is if you're in your boat and you're sort of up towards the equator or down towards Antarctica and you have an issue and either EPIRB goes off or you use various communications methods, we're the ones sitting on the other end of the line twenty-four seven answering, helping, sort of moving through to coordinating.

And typically, we get about roughly five safety of life calls a week that come through our center. So it's a significant activity that we're doing. We're very, very proud to do that. We have a relatively large team across New Zealand, which looks after all of our assets. So if you look up on the top of the mountains, many parts of New Zealand, you see those big, tall lattice towers.

They're typically owned by Cordia, and we have our communications equipment sitting across those. And we have teams spread out across New Zealand to manage those. But only about a third of their time is actually spent working on our equipment. We actually use them to provide services to other entities, be they New Zealand Defense Force, airways, MetService, customers like those that own typical infrastructure like Cordia does, and we help them from a maintenance perspective.

And then sort of the fifth area is we have four operation centers. So I talked about the two maritime operation centers. We also have a security operation center, which is attached to our cyber division, and we have a network operation center which manages our network, and we're actually starting to move to selling that as a service to other customers as well.

So that's sort of the breadth of what Cordia does. One of the aspects, and I suspect will be a point of discussion in this half hour that we did in FY '25, is we enlisted a company called Polis to do a detailed operational review of the company. I joined Cordia as a CEO on an interim basis in April of 2024. One of the first things the board asked me to do was really to do a detailed review of how Cordia is performing, how we are

structured internally, what the market opportunities are. And we brought Polis in. I won't go too much into that because I suspect there might be some questions around that. But there was a very detailed review with very, very specific outcomes, which we've then taken and are actually still implementing. And I can talk to that in as much detail as is needed.

And I thought I'd just highlight a couple of what I think are operational highlights that happened through the year. You've seen the financial outcomes that we had, which we're very, very proud of, and we worked very hard to achieve those, which included removing almost $9 million of cost and wastage from the business.

But highlights for me through the year, outside the financial results, were re-signing the contract with Maritime New Zealand for another 10 years. As I said, we're very proud to provide that service. And it's a core service for us, and I think it goes to the DNA of who we are. We assisted with the Vanuatu earthquake.

When that happened at Christmastime, I remember very well getting the phone call from Feels Like people that had been asked to join New Zealand Defense Force to go across to lift the communications that had been decimated. And last week, actually, I was at a Pacific Islands conference. We do quite a lot on the Pacific Islands as well to really support our Pacific Island neighbors.

A lot of the technology that we've deployed in New Zealand is now being rolled out in the Pacific Islands, and we really spend time helping those customers. We also won the contract to build the cell phone coverage inside Te Kaha Stadium in Christchurch, the new stadium that's just been opened. That was a very competitive bid, and we're very, very proud to have won that. And we've actually, obviously beyond FY '25, but we've just handed that over before it went live, and it was on time and on budget. And actually, the

performance outstrips the requirements. So we're very proud of that. I obviously can't and won't go into the cybersecurity events, but there was numerous cybersecurity events that we helped customers and also non-customers. So quite often we'll get a knock on the door at about 6:00 PM on a Friday, tends to be when they happen, when an event has happened and people come to us and ask for our assistance, and we have a team ready to help them.

And we also were a key part of broadcasting many, many sporting events and cultural events through our media broadcast team. So I just wanted to give that as a little bit of a frame up, and hopefully that's been helpful. And happy to take any questions. Yeah. Thank you so much for that overview.

And first question from Ruben Davidson Thank you, Madam Chair. And I just wanted to acknowledge, in a previous career in broadcasting, I saw the commitment Claudia's team had to climbing up mountains with microwave links to bounce signals across Mountable ridgetops to get the pictures and sound out. So very aware of the amazing work your team does out in the field to do that.

Wanted to jump straight into the financial performance, which you've mentioned, and the decision in this financial year not to pay a dividend. So I just wanted to dig into that a little more, and also to look at what the forecast or target is for the next financial year as far as whether you expect to be investing back or returning a dividend.

So in FY25, we did pay a dividend. No, no. We're paying the dividend- Yes... this year. So we've just declared a dividend for the interim dividend for the current financial year of two and a half million. Mm-hmm. So for the FY25 year, we were carrying the financial consequences of exiting out of the PSN contract- Yes... which put us into a position where we weren't able to pay a dividend.

But we have turned that around, and the financial performance is back on track. Cool. All good. Lawrence Fan. Thank you, Madam Chair. Thank you so much for coming. Thank you. I want to check, so one of the things that you mentioned is around the environmental impact of Cordia, and I was looking at, I think, page 40... 37 of your report- Mm-hmm... talking about environmental impact.

Can I just check, I also noticed that you are scaling up the use of AI technology and et cetera, as you know, out of necessity. Mm-hmm. Do you factor in the use of AI and energy consumption of AI into your environmental impact and emissions? Not currently. But we're still, I would say, in the early stages of spinning up AI. So we have specific use cases that we're using it for, but we're not using it en masse.

And we have tools that have AI built into it that we utilize in our operation centers especially. Yeah. And obviously we measure the electricity requirements from that, but we don't specifically measure the environmental impacts of AI as of yet.

But that would be scope three. So we- Correct we're very good in terms of scope one and scope two. Yeah. But we haven't gone into detail in terms of scope three at this stage. Just checking, what's the rough timeframe for scope three? I think- Is there one? Yeah. No, I don't know that there is because the complexity of it and the changes to the expectations around reporting in this regard, in terms of what's feasible and the double counting that occurs when everyone's doing scope three.

So yeah, when you guys come up with a solution on that, we'll happy to follow it. Okay then. Thank you so much. We follow the legislation that's required. Yeah. Yes. Thank you. All right. Supplementary from Reuben Edwards. Yeah, just supping on the question around AI. Mm-hmm. And I imagine there'll be a few more questions on this topic. But looking at the annual report, page 13, there's a reference to a number of support and governance documents which are then listed as bullets.

And runs through a whole range of issues and considerations for staff and your team. There's no specific reference in there to any policy around AI or internal policy around AI, which surprised me. But is that included within one of those? We do have one now. You're right. Yeah. So as part of the journey of AI, we have, as a board, put in place the AI policy.

So when- And as you can imagine, you have to look at it very regularly because things are changing. You know, when we first put it in place, we were saying, "You can only use these tools," and very rapidly that became attainable. Yeah. So we have it on a much more regular cadence of reviewing it. And we also brought onto the board in the more recent round, an individual who has a very strong background in AI.

So from a governance perspective, we're all learning, but I think we're doing the best we can. So when was that policy initiated? Well, it would've been in this current financial year- This-... that it got put in place. Yeah. And what's the process for...

Because you say correctly that these things keep getting updated. Yeah. What's the process for making sure everybody knows that that policy's getting updated? So all policies, so one of the things that we've put in place is a system called Highvol, which is our HRSS system, which manages all communications to staff.

And through that, we release policies and all staff, once policies are released, have to go and read it and tick and identify that they've actually read it and at times do a little mini test just to make sure that they understand what the policy is. And all that's tracked. Mm-hmm. So that's for all policies that go through. All right. Arena Williams.

Thank you. And thank you all for joining us this morning. I'm really interested in the way that the group is changing- Mm-hmm... and the divestment of the managed IT business to Fusion 5. Mm-hmm. And I see how that fits in with the statements around a new purpose and trying to understand how you see the group changing in the next couple of years, in response to the work that you have done.

And which parts of these are critical assets to New Zealand, which parts of these are part of the strategic monopoly assets- Mm-hmm... that are really important for public ownership. And which parts do you see as, say the managed IT business, it would've been hard for me to make a case to New Zealanders that that might have been sort of a critical monopoly asset. So talk to me about what this looks like over the next couple of years.

Yeah, no, very good question. We spent a lot of time working through this and thinking it, because it's really important for Courtards, as I think it is every business, to have a very clear sense of who you are and what you want to achieve, and the why. Why you exist, for the staff, the owner, and your customers.

Our focus is around critical communications infrastructure and cyber resilience. That's our North Star. And really, the way we look at that is we're looking for entities and customers where consequences is actually the issue, not convenience. So if there's a customer that, for example, obviously safety of life, there's a huge consequence attached to that if we don't do that well.

Broadcast, if we are not broadcasting as needed, again, there's a huge consequence. Cybersecurity, if we're not keeping cybersecurity customers safe, there's a huge consequence. That's how we measure what we do. Neil, if I can touch a- Please... little bit on the history. So, Cordia has come out of the media and broadcast space.

Mm-hmm. And everyone knows that digital terrestrial television is on a gradual downward slope, and the broadcast is increasingly using internet in terms of how they're communicating and putting everything out. So from a board perspective, when you look at that, you say, "Well, we have fixed costs and a declining revenue line." So over the years, there's been a lot of work done to diversify the business.

Mm-hmm. And if you look back, you can see this is run like that. And-... now they're gradually paring back to work out where are the areas where they can be successful and have sources of competitive advantage and an ability to be successful. Mm-hmm. You asked the question about what needs to be critically owned by- The people... the New Zealand government and the people.

Yeah. Mm-hmm. That's a really hard one because, in some instances, the things that we do, being Crown-owned is constraining because we not necessarily have the capital to invest into scale and growth and so forth. But in other instances, having the Cordia brand and reputation helps enormously in terms of trust and so forth.

It's up to the shareholders to decide- Yeah... what they want to do with Cordia. In Australia, BAI, which is the equivalent, was sold over a decade ago. And they are performing really well and have expanded enormously because they've got private capital in them. It's a different story here in terms of what we're doing.

But also, you have to take into account, for example, Cordia's communications were critical in Gabrielle, in Kaikoura- Mm-hmm... recent weather events. So it's not an easy answer. Just- All right. Just one quick follow-up. Robin and I have recently met with TechNZ, FinTechNZ. They're all saying, and I'm sure they're saying to all political parties, there is some critical infrastructure here which New Zealand is missing.

The bridges and the roads of the future that are those digital connections, things like an API center to actually use between parties who want to compete in finance- Okay... and in the digital age, but cannot because we don't have that infrastructure here. Are you looking at the other pieces of critical digital infrastructure that do make sense for New Zealanders to own?

Yeah. The short answer is yes. So we're looking forward. We're looking at data sovereignty, for example, as a key thought process that we're going through. We're looking at an example of that is if you think about our sites around the country at the moment, with the large towers, they typically have a large building at the bottom of them.

Yeah. Those large buildings used to be very full of analog equipment. They're now largely empty because it's digital. But you have this wonderful computer space which has resilience, which has connectivity, and we're thinking about how can we use that in the best way for Cordia and for New Zealand, especially when you think about data sovereignty.

We're thinking about the advances in space and LEO satellites, what's happening there. Mm-hmm. What space Cordia can play in that. One of the things that I didn't talk about that we do is we actually manage the Warkworth satellite station for Spark New Zealand, so we have expertise in that area. So we're looking at that and understanding what does that look like, what is this massive growth in, and I actually think a fundamental competitive advantage New Zealand has in this area because of where we're positioned, and the land that we have and the infrastructure we have. So we're looking at that.

So there's various areas that we're looking at to understand what does Cordia of the future look like. I think your point was really excellent. It's about deciding what is the critical infrastructure and assets that Cordia needs to own, and how do we leverage that for the future. And if I can just add to that. I think the most strategic asset that Cordia owns is our digital microwave radio network.

It's nationwide. It was built 25 years ago. We're just upgrading it now. It was built to transport the media content to our sites to distribute, but it's the most probably resilient network in the country. Mm-hmm. It stayed up with the Kaikoura earthquake, Christchurch earthquake, the recent cyclones, and we've partnered with the telcos to actually transport some of their mobile tech- Mm-hmm... on that network too. So for us, that's very strategic and plays to that mission-critical resilience.

Mm-hmm. Right. We'll go to Dr. Vanessa Banning. Thank you, Madam Chair. Thanks very much. It's good to see you. We've been reading about you over the term of this parliament, but not necessarily getting to see you, so it's good. So the renewal of that 10-year Maritime New Zealand contract's obviously a really important piece, not just for service outcomes, but it looks to me as though that would be underpinning some of the utilization of your other assets across your network to the longer term legacy, that thing. So given that that's a

long-term public contract, how important is that to have that for that long-term duration to be able to then underpin your ability to then continue to invest in things like cloud and cyber, whilst still maintaining that legacy but safety for infrastructure?

Yeah. It's very important. I think from memory, the maritime network utilizes around 54 of our sites. And so that long-term contract does provide, yeah, a lot of scale for those other things. On those sites also, we have a lot of FM services.

And we believe that also has a huge life on it, buoyed by the fact that the Crown has recently obviously provided those 20-year licenses. But also the fact that New Zealand has a very long vehicle turnover, 14.8 years. So not just the maritime, but I think FM and obviously digital television, which we recently signed TVNZ up for several more years. All those things do play a big part in allowing us to leverage those assets into similar things across infrastructure and mission-critical resilience.

Can I just ask something? So given that you are a, I don't know if you'll be able to answer this. Given you are a state-owned enterprise, wholly state-owned, do you think that plays a part in winning some of those longer-term public contracts? Is that-- Would a- No. If you were to be a public- You need the assets to win them, so. Yeah. And because I got asked this by somebody else a little while ago, and I think the example to point to that is we also won the same for Australia.

Yeah. And obviously another state-owned enterprise in Australia. So I think it's our capability and our proven track record that really made the difference. Great, thank you. Dr. David Wilson. The primary is... Cheers. Yes. The primary is really pleased to see you're moving into AI, right down to agentic AI, which means we can make this thing work without somebody on the other end necessarily, but just checking.

So you're on a journey there. Mm-hmm. The two things that spring to mind here is how's that going, and the second thing that springs to mind in terms of your security operations center is how is that integrated with the National Security Center, exchanging information and working together? So yes, definitely on a journey with AI, as I think we all are.

We've taken a slightly different approach with AI to possibly traditional. So our head of AI is also actually the head of people and culture. So rather than taking it from a technology lens, we're taking it from a how do we use AI to allow people to do their job better or actually to do more valuable work, rather than it's a pure technology, let's roll it out and everybody has to use it approach.

Yeah. And that's really gaining momentum because it means that the examples of AI become a lot more almost business case focused, where we can actually do a better job, save more money, or do things better faster. If we think about our operation center, AI is a key tool that we use in those.

There's absolutely huge leaps and bounds in capability that's happening there almost on a weekly basis. We do that largely through the tools that we use. There's several key tools that we use to monitor. So if we think about operation center as eyes on glass, where you're monitoring everything that's happening. Being able to use AI to do that and actually surface up the information for key people to take action on as opposed to have a range of people. It also allows us to scale because it means for every new customer, we don't need to bring on new people.

So that's a key part. And to your final question, yes, we work very closely with the National Security Center. We pass information backwards and forwards on a regular basis. We have people with clearances to have the appropriate conversations, and they happen on a regular basis.

So in this center, it's a matter of can we afford not to. Yes. Yeah. Absolutely. My second question was your comments around microwave and the towers and reaching into regional areas. And so, I guess what I'm asking here is how successful have we been or could we be in terms of reaching far out places, regional areas that have got no connectivity, and not just in an emergency- Yes... but full stop to allow them to take advantage of what's happening now.

Do you want to take that? Yeah. Very successful. I mean, we have a number of digital microwave radio-only solutions for customers, particularly in those agricultural regions and regions where fiber isn't as efficient. The DMR radio network I was talking about is a trunk line, and then off that trunk line, then we can have more specific, targeted DMR radio to service any customer anywhere.

And that's also complemented now by satellite Starlink. So we utilize that form of connectivity as well. Yeah. So it's integrated with different modes. Yeah. Giving more and more of that as well. That's amazing. Last thing I'll just say, congratulations on your turnaround. Thank you. Financially. All right. Supplementary from Lawrence Yuna.

Just a very quick supplementary. On the connectivity in rural areas, one of the things we've heard is the concern around this establishment decommissioning 3G. Just when you're talking about Starlink, can I just check that those level of connectivity is able to be utilized in all weather patterns?

Well, there'd be some weather patterns, some storms that may knock out that connectivity. And so that's why we ensure that we have at least two forms of connectivity complemented by Starlink. The primary is normally the digital microwave radio network or the fiber network. But yeah, there's always going to be something that potentially can knock out Starlink in space.

New question from Ruben Davidson. Thank you, Madam Chair. The minister's '24, '25 letter of expectation encouraged you to You know, position yourself for a challenging environment, but also to stay responsive to industry changes and, the thing that I'm really interested in, support media customers through difficult conditions.

So how are you supporting media customers? Collaborative approach with TVNZ to look at extending their digital television contracts with Kordia. The negotiation probably took eight months. Weekly meetings with their senior team and our senior team, looking at their challenges in respect to operating multiple platforms. So as you well know, they developed a digital platform which adds more cost to their business.

They're trying to move their customers across the legacy platforms to the digital ones. And so we've developed a commercial model that helps them with that while giving us the tenure, and a definitive period of term. So yeah, it's creative, and I think both parties at the end of that negotiation were satisfied with those outcomes, giving TVNZ the ability to manage their costs and, for Kordia to extend the term several more years.

Just to add on to that, the other thing that we're doing is we're working with a company in the UK called Arqiva, which specializes in software products that help media entities move into the cloud, put content through the cloud, and do that in a very cost-effective way.

So we're actually bringing those products to market to help the media companies actually make the transition. So rather than stopping them, we're actually working with them to help them make the transition that they need to make. Yeah, great. And are you- A very quick sup on that. Beyond TVNZ, are there any other companies that you've been working with in a larger way than the cloud migration potentially- Absolutely... and if so, how are you tracking and reporting who you're working with and therefore who you're not?

We're working with all our digital television customers right now. A lot of work's going in with Sky following the acquisition of Warner Brothers Discovery. And yeah, those negotiations are ongoing. Great. We'll have to conclude this hearing here. Thank you so much for your time. You might receive some questions later on. Thank you. Thank you.
Thank you very much for the opportunity. Thank you. Members, we move into private session now.
